<findings>

[observation] [warning] Contains 43% of project files (19/44) — may be too broad, consider splitting
[observation] [info] 11 additional files beyond the listed 8 suggest significant breadth — audit whether all utilities are actively consumed or whether dead code has accumulated here.
[observation] [warning] Cohesion of 0.56 is below the strong-cohesion threshold, indicating this zone bundles heterogeneous utilities; splitting auth/exec/output into focused sub-modules would improve navigability.
[observation] [info] project-config.ts and project-dirs.ts serving as 'types' alongside runtime utilities like exec.ts indicates mixed abstraction levels within this zone.
[pattern] [warning] The support zone plays two conflicting roles: foundation (23 imports into core treat it as a lower layer) and utility consumer (3 reverse imports from core treat it as a higher layer) — this dual-role is the root cause of the bidirectional coupling and should drive the refactor boundary.
[relationship] [warning] llm-runtime-support is entirely absent from the test import graph — all 25 test-to-source imports target llm-provider-core exclusively, leaving the largest zone in the package with zero unit test coverage.
[anti-pattern] [warning] auth.ts coordinates config resolution (resolveApiKey, resolveCliPath) and process execution (exec) to authenticate providers — this is provider-level orchestration logic, not a standalone utility. Placing it in the support zone alongside pure utilities (json.ts, output.ts, project-dirs.ts) inflates the zone's apparent cohesion score and obscures the architectural role of authentication.
[anti-pattern] [critical] config.ts is imported by 3 files in the core zone (api-provider.ts, cli-provider.ts, create-client.ts) and 1 file in the support zone (auth.ts) — it serves the core zone more than the support zone. Its physical placement in support is the sole cause of the bidirectional coupling; relocating it to the core zone would reduce the architectural risk to zero without changing any logic.
[suggestion] [warning] AuthMode (types.ts) and ProviderAuthMode (provider-interface.ts) are duplicate auth-mode types — AuthMode should be replaced with ProviderAuthMode or aliased to it so ClaudeClient.mode and ProviderInfo.mode share the same type. The current mismatch makes ClaudeClient structurally incompatible with LLMProvider even for equivalent values.
[suggestion] [info] The OPENAI_API_KEY retry-without-key workaround in codex-cli-provider.ts embeds an undocumented environmental assumption about Codex CLI credential precedence — document this behavior in a JSDoc block or inline comment at the point where CodexConfig is defined in llm-types.ts so downstream consumers know the env interaction exists.
[suggestion] [info] codex-cli-provider.ts hardcodes DEFAULT_CODEX_MODEL without a config.ts counterpart — add a resolveCodexModel utility to config.ts parallel to resolveModel() for Claude to establish a single authoritative default-model resolution path per vendor.

</findings>

<insights>

- Contains 43% of project files (19/44) — may be too broad, consider splitting
- Moderate cohesion (0.56) suggests this zone is a collection of loosely related utilities rather than a single coherent concern; consider whether auth, exec, and output belong in separate modules.
- With 23 imports into llm-provider-core, this zone acts as a dependency on core types/interfaces, which is the correct direction for a support layer.
- The single entry point (src/config.ts) may be underdeclaring the zone's public surface; utilities like auth.ts and exec.ts are likely consumed directly by providers.
- Cohesion of 0.56 is below the strong-cohesion threshold, indicating this zone bundles heterogeneous utilities; splitting auth/exec/output into focused sub-modules would improve navigability.
- 11 additional files beyond the listed 8 suggest significant breadth — audit whether all utilities are actively consumed or whether dead code has accumulated here.
- project-config.ts and project-dirs.ts serving as 'types' alongside runtime utilities like exec.ts indicates mixed abstraction levels within this zone.
- The support zone has no test coverage visible in the import graph (tests/unit/ imports exclusively target llm-provider-core), meaning the 19-file support zone is entirely untested — this is the largest test coverage gap in the package.
- The support zone's role as a dependency of core (23 imports upward) while also receiving 3 imports back from core means it simultaneously acts as a foundation layer and a utility layer — these two roles should be separated to resolve the bidirectional coupling.
- llm-runtime-support is entirely absent from the test import graph — all 25 test-to-source imports target llm-provider-core exclusively, leaving the largest zone in the package with zero unit test coverage.
- The support zone plays two conflicting roles: foundation (23 imports into core treat it as a lower layer) and utility consumer (3 reverse imports from core treat it as a higher layer) — this dual-role is the root cause of the bidirectional coupling and should drive the refactor boundary.
- exec.ts explicitly documents in JSDoc that streaming consumers should use spawn directly from node:child_process — the two CLI providers bypassing exec.ts for their spawn calls is by design, not an oversight. exec.ts is correctly scoped to non-streaming subprocess orchestration.
- auth.ts is the only file in the support zone that orchestrates multiple other support utilities (config.ts + exec.ts) rather than providing a standalone utility — it operates at a higher abstraction level than the other files in this zone, suggesting it is a provider-level concern misplaced in the utility layer.
- auth.ts coordinates config resolution (resolveApiKey, resolveCliPath) and process execution (exec) to authenticate providers — this is provider-level orchestration logic, not a standalone utility. Placing it in the support zone alongside pure utilities (json.ts, output.ts, project-dirs.ts) inflates the zone's apparent cohesion score and obscures the architectural role of authentication.
- config.ts is imported by 3 files in the core zone (api-provider.ts, cli-provider.ts, create-client.ts) and 1 file in the support zone (auth.ts) — it serves the core zone more than the support zone. Its physical placement in support is the sole cause of the bidirectional coupling; relocating it to the core zone would reduce the architectural risk to zero without changing any logic.
- AuthMode (types.ts) and ProviderAuthMode (provider-interface.ts) are parallel auth-mode types serving the same semantic role but with different extensibility: AuthMode is a closed union ('api' | 'cli') while ProviderAuthMode is open-ended. ClaudeClient.mode is typed to AuthMode and LLMProvider.info.mode to ProviderAuthMode, preventing structural compatibility between the two interfaces without a cast even when the value is identical.
- codex-cli-provider.ts hardcodes DEFAULT_CODEX_MODEL = 'gpt-5-codex' directly in the provider file with no counterpart in config.ts or llm-config.ts where Claude default model constants live — the two vendors follow divergent conventions for default model resolution.
- The OPENAI_API_KEY environment variable stripping in codex-cli-provider.ts is an undocumented external dependency: if OPENAI_API_KEY is present in the environment, it overrides Codex CLI session credentials, causing auth failures that are silently retried with the key removed. This environmental assumption is not documented in config.ts or llm-config.ts where other env-var conventions are described.
- AuthMode (types.ts) and ProviderAuthMode (provider-interface.ts) are duplicate auth-mode types — AuthMode should be replaced with ProviderAuthMode or aliased to it so ClaudeClient.mode and ProviderInfo.mode share the same type. The current mismatch makes ClaudeClient structurally incompatible with LLMProvider even for equivalent values.
- codex-cli-provider.ts hardcodes DEFAULT_CODEX_MODEL without a config.ts counterpart — add a resolveCodexModel utility to config.ts parallel to resolveModel() for Claude to establish a single authoritative default-model resolution path per vendor.
- The OPENAI_API_KEY retry-without-key workaround in codex-cli-provider.ts embeds an undocumented environmental assumption about Codex CLI credential precedence — document this behavior in a JSDoc block or inline comment at the point where CodexConfig is defined in llm-types.ts so downstream consumers know the env interaction exists.
- [call graph] 269 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 4 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

</insights>
